coredata.dc:title: Boron-doped Graphene quantum dots modified electrode for electrochemistry and electrocatalysis of hemoglobin
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Wei
Weng, Wenju
Niu, Xueliang
Li, Xiaoyan
Men, Yongling
Sun, Wei
Li, Guangjiu
Dong, Lifeng
coredata.dc:description: In this paper boron-doped graphene quantum dots (B-GQDs) decorated carbon ionic liquid electrode (CILE) was fabricated for the immobilization of hemoglobin (Hb), which was used as the electrochemical biosensor for electrocatalysis. Spectroscopic results demonstrated that the original structure of Hb was unchanged after mixed with B-GQDs, indicating the biocompatibility of B-GQDs. Electrochemical behaviors of Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE were investigated in pH 4.0 phosphate buffer solution and a couple of well-distinct symmetric redox peaks appeared on cyclic voltammograms. The results were also compared with GQDs and nitrogen-doped GQDs modified electrodes, which illustrated the enhanced effects of B-GQDs to electron communication and loading capability of Hb on the electrode surface due to its high conductivity and small size. Electrochemical kinetics were studied with the electrochemical parameters calculated. Electrocatalysis of Hb and B-GQDs modified electrode to various substrates such as trichloroacetic acid, sodium nitrite and hydrogen peroxide were carefully investigated. This new Hb modified electrode exhibited the advantages of large linear relationship, high sensitivity, low detection limit, good anti-interference ability and excellent stability, which found wide applications in food and drug samples detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boron-doped graphene quantum dots
Hemoglobin
Direct electrochemistry
Electrocatalytic behavior

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Science and Technology Program of Haikou City

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21665007 ), the Hainan Province National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 2017CXTD007 ), the Key Science and Technology Program of Haikou City ( 2017042 ), Foundation of Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker of Ministry of Education of Qingdao University of Science and Technology ( STAM201808 ) and the Graduate Student Innovation Research Project of Hainan Province ( Hys2017-149 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of Hb in water solution (a) and pH 4.0 PBS (c), and Hb-B-GQDs mixture in pH 4.0 PBS (b); (B) FT-IR spectra of Hb (a) and Hb-B-GQDs composite film (b).

SEM images of (A) CILE and (B) B-GQDs/CILE; (C) EIS of Nafion/B-GQDs/CILE (a), Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE (b), Nafion/CILE (c) and Nafion/Hb/CILE (d) in a 10.0 mmol·L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 mol·L−1 KCl solution with frequency from 104 to 0.1 Hz.

Cyclic voltammograms of Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE (a), Nafion/Hb/GQDs/CILE (b), Nafion/Hb/CILE (c), Nafion/Hb/N-GQDs/CILE (d), Nafion/B-GQDs/CILE (e), Nafion/CILE (f) and CILE (g) in pH 4.0 PBS at scan rate of 0.1 V·s−1.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE at different scan rates (from a to k as 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900, 1000 mV·s−1) in pH 4.0 PBS; (B) Linear relationships of the redox peak currents vs scan rate (υ); (C) Linear relationships of the redox peak potentials vs logυ.

(A) Influence of buffer pH on cyclic voltammograms of Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE at the scan rate of 0.1 V·s−1 (from a to h as 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9); (B) Linear relationship between E0′ and pH (from 2 to 9).

Cyclic voltammograms of (A) Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE with TCA (from a to k as 5.0, 50.0, 100.0, 150.0, 200.0, 260.0, 300.0, 360.0, 400.0, 440.0, 460.0 mmol·L−1; inset was the linear relationship between TCA concentration and currents); (B) Nafion/B-GQDs/CILE with TCA (from a to e as 0, 50.0, 100.0, 150.0, 200.0 mmol·L−1) in pH 4.0 PBS at the scan rate of 100 mV·s−1. (C) Differential pulse voltammograms of TCA on Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE with various concentration (from a to k as 0.1, 10.0, 50.0, 80.0, 150.0, 200.0, 250.0, 300.0, 350.0, 400.0, 450.0 mmol·L−1).

Cyclic voltammograms of (A) Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE with NaNO2 (from a to m as 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0 50.0, 60.0, 70.0, 80.0, 90.0, 100.0 mmol·L−1; inset was the linear relationship between NaNO2 concentration and currents); (B) Nafion/B-GQDs/CILE with NaNO2 (from a to e as 0, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0 mmol·L−1) in pH 4.0 PBS at the scan rate of 100 mV·s−1. (C) DPV curves for NaNO2 detection (from a to k as 0.1, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, 50.0, 60.0, 70.0, 80.0, 90.0, 100.0 mmol·L−1).

Cyclic voltammograms of (A) Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE with H2O2 (from a to n as 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 10.0, 12.0, 14.0, 16.0, 18.0, 20.0, 22.0, 24.0, 26.0 mmol·L−1; inset was the linear relationship between H2O2 concentration and currents). (B) Nafion/B-GQDs/CILE with H2O2 (4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0 mmol·L−1) in pH 4.0 PBS at the scan rate of 100 mV·s−1. (C) DPV curves for H2O2 detection (from a to i as 0.0, 1.0, 5.0, 8.0, 10.0, 13.0, 18.0, 23.0, 26.0 mmol·L−1).

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE for (A) TCA detection (curves a to e represented as PBS, medical facial peel sample solution, and the addition of 20.0, 40.0, 60.0 mmol·L−1 TCA standard solution) and (B) NaNO2 detection (curves a to e represented as PBS, soak water sample solution, and the addition of 10.0, 20.0, 30.0 mmol·L−1 NaNO2 standard solution).
80 cycles overlapped cyclic voltammograms of Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE in pH 4.0 PBS at the scan rate of 100 mV·s−1.
Detailed fabrication process of CILE and modification procedure with helical conformation of Hb and electrochemical active center displayed as ferroporphyrin.
Comparison of electrochemical parameters of different Hb modified electrodes to TCA detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *ASPCE, activated screen printed carbon electrode; GR, graphene; CTS, chitoson; CNT, carbon nanotubes; CMS, carbon microspheres.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results of real samples by the proposed method (n = 3).
Interference effects of foreign ions and compounds to Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE in pH 4.0 PBS.

head.ce:title: Boron-doped Graphene quantum dots modified electrode for electrochemistry and electrocatalysis of hemoglobin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Photoelectrochemistry of Haikou, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hainan Normal University, Haikou 571158, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Photoelectrochemistry of Haikou
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Hamline University, Hewitt Avenue, Saint Paul, MN, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper boron-doped graphene quantum dots (B-GQDs) decorated carbon ionic liquid electrode (CILE) was fabricated for the immobilization of hemoglobin (Hb), which was used as the electrochemical biosensor for electrocatalysis. Spectroscopic results demonstrated that the original structure of Hb was unchanged after mixed with B-GQDs, indicating the biocompatibility of B-GQDs. Electrochemical behaviors of Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE were investigated in pH 4.0 phosphate buffer solution and a couple of well-distinct symmetric redox peaks appeared on cyclic voltammograms. The results were also compared with GQDs and nitrogen-doped GQDs modified electrodes, which illustrated the enhanced effects of B-GQDs to electron communication and loading capability of Hb on the electrode surface due to its high conductivity and small size. Electrochemical kinetics were studied with the electrochemical parameters calculated. Electrocatalysis of Hb and B-GQDs modified electrode to various substrates such as trichloroacetic acid, sodium nitrite and hydrogen peroxide were carefully investigated. This new Hb modified electrode exhibited the advantages of large linear relationship, high sensitivity, low detection limit, good anti-interference ability and excellent stability, which found wide applications in food and drug samples detection.

ce:section.ce:para: As a commonly used redox protein, hemoglobin (Hb) has the biological functions of oxygen storage and transportation with its secondary structure observed in Scheme 1 . There are two α and two β chains twined and folded together to form a spherical shape, which has one ferroporphyrin inside of each three-dimensional chain. This ferroporphyrin has been denominated to be the electroactive center that takes part in the redox reaction to lose and obtain electrons [1]. Therefore Hb is often selected as an exemplar molecule in the fields of direct electrochemistry and electrochemical biosensing [2]. However, ferroporphyrins are wrapped by biomacromolecules and hardly to transfer electrons outside the three-dimensional structure [3]. Therefore a diverse range of modifiers and membranes have been designed to solve this problem [4–10]. For example Palanisamy et al. applied various nanomaterials such as zinc oxide nanopartides, silver nanoparticles, multi-walled carbon nanotubes‑zinc oxide nanocomposite, graphene-β-cyclodextrin nanocomposite for the direct electrochemistry of redox proteins [11–14].

Carbon based nanomaterials have attracted much attentions due to their special physicochemical properties and diversiform shapes. Graphene (GR), which stands for two-dimensional nanosheets composed of carbon hexatomic rings with its definition usually recognized as a carbon of one-atom-layered thickness, has been the main focus of research during the past few years. Based on its unique planar structure, GR can bow and wrap into zero-dimensional fullerene [15], curl into one-dimensional carbon nanotubes [16], or pile up to three-dimensional graphite [17]. By manipulating the size and shape of nanomaterials, various GR derivatives have been explored with different chemical, physical and electronic properties [18]. Tremendous applications focused on biosensing, biomedicine, energy-related devices and nanodevices have also been extensively employed [19, 20]. GR quantum dots (GQDs) are defined as small-sized GR, whose transverse dimension is smaller than 100 nm with longitudinal dimension as single-layered, double-layered or multi-layered. GQDs possess very strong quantum confinement and edge effects with the native properties of GR retained [21]. Compared with conventional semiconductive quantum dots, GQDs have more advantages such as excellent biocompatibility, ease of combining with biomolecules and low biological toxicity [22]. Meanwhile, GQDs are easy to be chemically modified with the superior conductivity and large specific area retained [23]. Samuei et al. synthesized GQDs/CoNiAl layered double-hydroxide nanocomposite modified electrode to build a non-enzymatic glucose sensor [24]. Razmi et al. immobilized glucose oxidase onto GQDs modified carbon ceramic electrode to design a biocompatible platform for glucose determination [25]. Zhang et al. constructed a hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) bioplatform with GQDs modified Au electrode [26]. To further improve the characteristics and expanding applications of GQDs, doping foreign atoms into GQDs had been explored, which endowed great changes including structure defect and the interaction of carbon atom with foreign atoms around it [27]. Due to the close atomic radius of boron (B) atom and C atom, the C atoms in GR structure can be replaced by B atoms. B atom lacks one electron in outermost layer as compared to C atom, which exhibits electron-deficient property to some extent with the photoelectrochemical properties being fine-tuned. B-GODs have specific structure and unique electronic properties, which exhibit excellent electrocatalytic activity and facilitate the electron transfer [28–30]. Zhang et al. established a glucose detection method with the aggregation-induced photoluminescence enhancement existed in B-GQDs [31]. Qu et al. built a sensitive platform for glucose detection in microdialysate with boronic acid functionalized GQDs [32]. Fei et al. synthesized electrocatalysts consisting of GR and GQDs co-doped with boron and nitrogen [33].

In this paper B-doped GQDs were synthesized and utilized for the construction of protein modified platform with Hb as the model. By using Nafion film, B-GQDs and Hb were fixed on the surface of carbon ionic liquid electrode (CILE) to get a modified CILE (Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE), which acted as the working electrode in the electrochemical investigation. Nafion is a commonly used film forming polymer material in the electrode modification due to its high stability. This Hb modified electrode exhibited a couple of well-distinct redox peaks on cyclic voltammogram, demonstrating a fast electron communication of Hb on this bioplatform due to the incorporation of B-GQDs. Therefore direct electron transfer of Hb was accelerated on the modified electrode surface and electrochemical behaviors of Hb were checked in detail. Due to the mimic enzymatic structure of Hb, electrocatalysis of this Hb modified electrode was carefully investigated with the real samples detected by this proposed method. The detailed preparation procedure of this Hb and B-GQDs based electrode was illustrated in Scheme 1.

CHI 604E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instrument, China) was used for electrochemical experiments, which was connected with a modified electrode (Φ = 4.0 mm) as the working electrode, a platinum wire electrode as the auxiliary electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed on a JSM-7100F scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum and ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum were performed on Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA) and TU-1901 double-beam UV–visible spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., China), respectively.

1-Hexylpyridinium hexafluorophosphate (HPPF6) was purchased from Lanzhou Yulu Fine Chem. Ltd. Co. (China). GQDs, B-GQDs and nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) were prepared based on the reported procedure with their properties clearly described in the reference [27]. Graphite powder (average particle size 30 μm, Shanghai Colloid Chemical Co., China), bovine Hb (MW. 64500, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., China), trichloroacetic acid (TCA, Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Co., China), NaNO2 (Shanghai Chem. Plant, China) and H2O2 (Xilong Scientific Ltd. Co., China) were used as received. 0.1 mol·L−1 phosphate buffer solution (PBS) were selected as the supporting electrolyte, which was deoxygenized by N2 for 30 min just before experiment. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used directly.

CILE was got as the previously reported procedure with graphite power, HPPF6 and a glass electrode tube [34], which was smoothed before use. Then CILE was modified with B-GQDs, Hb and Nafion by drop-casting method in sequence with a microsyringe. In brief, 6.0 μL of B-GQDs (0.6 mg·mL−1), 8.0 μL of Hb (15.0 mg·mL−1) and 8 μL of 0.5% Nafion were applied to the surface of CILE every 2-hour interval in turn and dried at room temperature in air. Lastly the modified electrode was taken out and rinsed with water, which was denominated as Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE. Other working electrodes like Nafion/Hb/N-GQDs/CILE, Nafion/Hb/GQDs/CILE, Nafion/Hb/CILE, Nafion/B-GQDs/CILE and Nafion/CILE were fabricated by the similar procedures (6 μL of 0.6 mg·mL−1 N-GQDs or GQDs were used as well). During drying, a small bottle was placed on the electrode to ensure the slowly evaporation of solution with a uniform film formed. All the working electrodes were stored in a 4 °C refrigerator when not used.

UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy is an effective method to monitor secondary biostructure of the proteins and the migration of the Soret band can provide the structural information [35]. Fig. 1A showed the UV–Vis spectra of Hb in water (curve a), Hb in pH 4.0 PBS (curve c) and Hb-B-GQDs mixture in pH 4.0 PBS (curve b), which gave the same typical Soret band of heme protein at 405.00 nm. Therefore Hb kept its original structure without any change after being mixed with B-GQDs in pH 4.0 PBS. FT-IR spectroscopy is also employed to exam the completeness of the secondary structure of the heme proteins [36], and the data can provide significantly spectroscopic information of protein. The peak at 1700–1600 cm−1 is caused by CO stretching vibrations and the peak at 1600–1500 cm−1 results from the contribution of both NH bending and CN stretching of protein groups [37]. The typical infrared absorption bands of Hb were observed at 1651 cm−1 and 1541 cm−1 (Fig. 1B, curve a), which was the same as that of Hb-B-GQDs composite film (Fig. 1B, curve b). Therefore Hb kept its native structure after being mixed with B-GQDs.

A series of B-GQDs with various concentration were used for the CILE modification with 6 μL and the electrochemical performances were checked with a 0.1 mmol·L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.5 mol·L−1 KCl solution by cyclic voltammetry. Experimental data showed that 0.6 mg·mL−1 of B-GQDs gave the highest voltammetric responses with best shape, which was used for the electrode modification. Nafion acts as the immobilization film in the fabrication of electrochemical modified electrodes and the presence of Nafion can fix molecules tightly on the electrode surface with the stability of Hb modified electrodes increased.

SEM images of CILE (A) and B-GQDs/CILE (B) were displayed in Fig.2 . On CILE an even surface could be observed, which was ascribed to the highly viscous IL that bound graphite powder tightly. After coating of B-GQDs on the CILE surface, many nano-islands can be found on the electrode surface. Because the sizes of B-GQDs were smaller than 100 nm, these nano-islands could be recognized as small-sized GR nanosheets that self-curled. Detailed information of B-GQDs used, such as high resolution transmission electron microscopy images, XRD and XPS etc. could be found in the reference [27].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a useful method for examining the properties of electrode surface and provides information on the impedance of the conductive interface. The semicircular part is related to the electron-transfer-limited process with diameter equivalent to the electron transfer resistance (Ret) [38]. Fig. 2C displayed EIS results of Nafion/B-GQDs/CILE (curve a), Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE (curve b), Nafion/CILE (curve c) and Nafion/Hb/CILE (curve d). The Ret value of Nafion/CILE was 85.2 Ω (curve c) with a standard deviation (SD) of 2.86%. By the incorporation of B-GQDs and Hb onto the electrode surface, the Ret values were changed to 37.4 Ω (SD = 4.3%, curve a) and 101.8 Ω (SD = 3.43%, curve d), respectively, which proved that the conductive B-GQDs and non-conductive Hb had the opposite effects on the interfacial resistances. As for Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE (curve b), the Ret value (60.3 Ω, SD = 2.36%) was in the middle of that of Nafion/B-GQDs/CILE and Nafion/Hb/CILE, proving the joint effects of different modifiers on the electrode.

As described in Fig. 3 , typical cyclic voltammograms of various film-modified electrodes were thoroughly explored. On CILE (g), Nafion/CILE (f) and Nafion/B-GQDs/CILE (e), no redox peaks could be observed, indicating that no electrochemical active substances existed with stable background currents. On Nafion/Hb/CILE (curve c) the appearance of a pair of asymmetrical redox peaks indicated that the electron communication of Hb was realized with slow rate. As for redox protein modified electrode, the realization of direct electrochemistry of redox protein is the research target. Due to the presence of heme iron porphyrin in the structure of Hb, electron transfer of Hb heme Fe(III) could take place with the formation of Hb heme Fe(II). However this reaction is difficult to be realized due to the slow electron transfer rate, caused by the deep bury of heme iron porphyrin in nonconductive peptide chains. Therefore various methods and materials are optimized for the enhancement of the electron transfer process of heme protein. On Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE (curve a) a couple of well-distinct symmetrical redox peaks were observed with amplified signals, which was attributed to the presence of B-GQDs on the electrode that fasten electron transfer rate between Hb molecules and electrode surface. For comparison GQDs and N-GQDs modified electrodes were fabricated with the electrochemical responses recorded. Both of them also exhibited enhance ability for Hb direct electrochemistry with a pair of redox peaks appeared. Also these peaks were larger than that of Nafion/Hb/CILE, indicating the positive effects of GQDs and doped-GQDs on the electron transfer of Hb. Due to the specific electronic properties and higher conductivity, the presence of GQDs related nanomaterials on the electrode surface show the enhancement of the communication of electroactive center with electrode. However, the differences of B, N and C atoms in the GQDs structure resulted in the variation of the bulk responses of the modified electrodes and in our experiment the B-GQDs gave the best response for Hb direct electrochemistry. The cathodic (Epc) and anodic (Epa) peak potentials were observed at −0.251 V and −0.165 V with the redox peak current increased 1.75 times than that of Nafion/Hb/CILE. The formal potential (E0′) was calculated to be −0.208 V (vs. SCE), which was the typical characteristic redox peak of Fe(III)/Fe(II) in heme groups. The ratio of redox currents was close to 1.0 and its peak-to-peak potential (ΔEp) was 86 mV, demonstrating a quasi-reversible process that existed on this redox protein sensing platform. This redox peak was originated from the electroactive center of Hb molecules and accelerated by B-GQDs on the electrode surface. Since Hb and B-GQDs were bulk modified on the CILE surface, the whole electrode was used as the working electrode for the further electrocatalytic investigation.

Effects of scan rate on cyclic voltammetric behaviors of Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE were investigated in pH 4.0 PBS from 0.05 to 1.00 V·s−1 with the curves overlapped in Fig. 4A. The roughly symmetric redox peak from the Hb molecules on the electrode surface appeared, and the redox peak potentials shifted slightly towards positive and negative terminals with the augments of scan rate along with the increase of ΔEp value simultaneously, indicating that the electron communication turned to be more irreversible under faster scan rate. Also the signals of redox peak amplified gradually with scan rate increased, proving that Hb experienced a typical interface-dominated process. Two linear regression equations were computed as Ipc (μA) = 62.9 υ (V·s−1) − 9.2 (n = 9, γ = 0.998) and Ipa (μA) = −78.8 υ (V·s−1) − 15.2 (n = 9, γ = 0.999), respectively (Fig. 4B). The relationships of redox potential with log υ were plotted with the regression equations as Epc (V) = −0.056 logυ (V·s−1) − 0.31 (n = 9, γ = 0.997) and Epa (V) = 0.053 logυ (V·s−1) − 0.13 (n = 9, γ = 0.998) (Fig. 4C). The charge transfer coefficient (α) and the apparent heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant (k
s ) were computed as 0.49 and 3.87 s−1. Therefore the presence of B-GQDs could build a favorable microenvironment for faster electron communication of Hb due to its specific properties. Furthermore, the apparent surface concentration (Γ*) of Hb was calculated by applying Faraday's formula: Q = nFAΓ* [39], where Q is for the charge, n for electron transfer number, F for Faraday's constant and A for effective area of this electrode. The effective area of B-GQDs/CILE (A) can be calculated by Randles-Sevcik [40]:Ipc = (2.69 × 105) n 3/2 AD 1/2 C*υ1/2, where Ipc is for reduction peak current of B-GQDs/CILE that scanned in potassium ferricyanide solution, D for coefficient of diffusion of potassium ferricyanide solution (0.76 × 10−5 cm2·s−1), C* for concentration of potassium ferricyanide solution (1.0 × 10−3 mol·L−1) and υ is for scan rate (0.1 V·s−1). Thus, A was calculated t as 0.161 cm2 (while geometrical area of CILE was 0.126 cm2). Then the Γ* of Hb in Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE was estimated as 1.76 × 10−9 mol·cm−2, accounted for 15.2% Hb on the electrode surface). Therefore the existence of B-GQDs offered more electron transfer bridges for the redox protein.

Electrochemical performance of hemin protein can be greatly affected by the pH of electrolyte solution. And the relationship of E0′ and pH can reflect the electrochemical process with proton participated. Effect of solution pH on cyclic voltammograms of Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE was recorded and shown in Fig.5A. The E0′ value shifted negatively with increasing pH of the electrolyte, which was due to the proton involved in the electron transfer from Hb Fe(III) to the reduced Hb Fe(II). The linear regression equation was given as E0′(V) = −0.0543 pH − 0.08139 (n = 8, γ = 0.990) with the slope value of −54.3 mV·pH−1, which was a little smaller than the theoretical data (−57.6 mV·pH −1) at 291 K for the reversible process involving one-electron transfer coupled to one-proton transportation [41]. And it is attributed to the effect from the protonation states of trans-ligands of the ferroporrphyrin and amino acids chains around the electroactive center, or the protonation of H2O coordinated to the ferroporrphyrin [42]. Therefore Hb is subject to a single-protonation process along with one electron transferred to electrochemical surface with equation as follows: Hb heme Fe(III) + H+ + e− ⇋ Hb heme Fe(II) [43]. Also the biggest current was observed at pH 4.0, which was chosen as the supporting electrolyte with enough protons provided for the electrochemical reaction.

Hb belongs to redox proteins that have the heme electroactive center, which exhibits the HRP enzymatic activity with the catalytic ability to various substrates. Therefore Hb can act as catalytic enzyme for the electrocatalytic reduction. Electrocatalysis of this Hb modified electrode to different substrates such as TCA, NaNO2 and H2O2 was carefully checked.

TCA is an organic halide that is utilized as a herbicide in agriculture and gardening, a peeling preparation in cosmetology and medical treatment, and an antiseptic in daily drinking water, which may result in environmental pollution. So a sensitive and flexible procedure for TCA detection is essential and significant [44]. However TCA is stable and hard to be dehalogenated on the normal working electrode, and the redox protein modified electrode can lower the active energy and fasten the reduction reaction. Electrocatalytic reduction towards TCA on Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE was investigated in detail with the results illustrated in Fig.6A. After TCA was added to pH 4.0 PBS, an increase of the reduction peak at −0.250 V was observed gradually with the oxidation peak disappeared, and the second reduction peak further appeared at −0.529 V with the continual increase of TCA concentration, indicating a typical electrocatalytic behavior conducted [45]. Among the TCA concentration from 7.0 to 420.0 mmol·L−1, the reduction peak current was proportionally increased with an equation as I (μA) = 0.401C (mmol·L−1) + 9.738 (n = 57, γ = 0.993) and the detection limit was calculated as 2.3 mmol·L−1 (3σ). When TCA concentration was more than 420.0 mmol·L−1, the catalytic current almost stayed invariant, exhibiting a Michaelis-Menten dynamic mechanism. The Lineweaver-Burk formula is used to value enzymatic affinity [46]: 1/I
ss = 1/I
max + K
M
app /(I
max ·C), where I
ss is for the steady state current detected in the linear range; I
max for the maximum current detected at the saturated substrate; C for the concentration of substrate. According to the concentration and the maximum peak current, the apparent Michaelis-Menten constant (K
M
app ) was calculated as 26.42 mmol·L−1. Also electrocatalysis of TCA on Nafion/B-GQDs/CILE was checked with the curves shown in Fig.6B, which gave a smaller direct catalytic current at higher TCA concentration. Therefore the presence of Hb exhibited excellent catalytic activity for the TCA electroreduction and B-GQDs exhibited great enhancements to the electrochemical response. To further increase the detection sensitivity, a typical differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was used for the TCA detection with the curves shown in Fig.6C. A sharp peak appeared with the linear range from 0.1 to 300.0 mmol·L−1 and linear regression equation as I (μA) = 0.469C (mmol·L−1) + 6.403 (n = 8, γ = 0.994). The detection limit was calculated as 0.053 mmol·L−1 (3σ), which was smaller than that of CV result. The comparison of the electrochemical and analytical parameters of this biosensor with other Hb modified electrodes was summarized in Table 1 . It can be seen that Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE had comparable linear range and detection limit.

NaNO2 is an important analytical target due to its food-borne toxicity that can be transferred into cancerigenic nitrosamine in human body. It can turn Hb into ferrihemoglobin and deprive the capability of carrying oxygen in human blood. Also NaNO2 has been utilized extensively in industry and food processing. Electrocatalytic performance of Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE towards different concentrations of NaNO2 was further checked with the curves shown in Fig.7A. A new reduction peak was found at −0.809 V and the reduction peak signal was linearly enhanced with NaNO2 concentration in two sections from 1.0 to 10.0 mmol·L−1 and 10.0 to 80.0 mmol·L−1. Two linear regression equations were presented as I (μA) = 11.27C (mmol·L−1) + 24.72 (n = 7, γ = 0.961) and I (μA) = 1.465C (mmol·L−1) + 122.28 (n = 9, γ = 0.991). The detection limit was given as 0.3 mmol·L−1 (3σ) and K
M
app was estimated as 6.372 mmol·L−1. Also Nafion/B-GQDs/CILE was used for the NaNO2 detection with cyclic voltammograms shown as Fig.7B, which had an electrocatalytic peak appeared at −1.05 V with smaller current values. Therefore the presence of Hb on the electrode can lower the reduction energy and increase the catalytic effects. DPV was also used for the sensitive detection of NaNO2 with the curves shown in Fig.7C. A linear range was got from 0.1 to 100.0 mmol·L−1 with the linear regression equation as I (μA) = 0.098C (mmol·L−1) + 6.282 (n = 11, γ = 0.997) and the detection limit as 0.03 mmol·L−1 (3σ).

Also electrocatalytic reduction activity towards H2O2 was explored by cyclic voltammetry with the data provided in Fig.8 . As for Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE (Fig.8A) the continuously addition of H2O2 in PBS resulted in the decrease of the oxidation peak and the reduction peak shifted gradually towards negative direction at −0.410 V. While on Nafion/B-GQDs/CILE the reduction peak of H2O2 appeared at −0.65 V (Fig.8B), the decrease of the reduction overpotential proved the biocatalytic function of Hb in the electrode reaction. A good linear relationship of H2O2 concentration with the reduction peak currents was got from 4.0 to 24.0 mmol·L−1 and the linear regression equation was I (μA) = 14.31C (mmol·L−1) − 12.94 (n = 12, γ = 0.996). The detection limit and K
M
app were given as 1.3 mmol·L−1 (3σ) and 26.60 mmol·L−1, respectively. Then DPV was utilized for the sensitive detection of H2O2 with the curves shown in Fig.8C. A linear range was got from 5.0 to 26.0 mmol·L−1 with the linear regression equation as I (μA) = 5.146C (mmol·L−1) − 22.602 (n = 7, γ = 0.991) and the detection limit as 0.04 mmol·L−1 (3σ).

Various samples containing TCA and NaNO2 were analyzed by this proposed method with typical cyclic voltammograms shown in Fig. 9 . The medical facials peel (35% TCA, Shanghai EKEAR Bio. Tech. Ltd. Co., China) was diluted by pH 4.0 PBS and used for TCA detection. Pickled vegetables were purchased from a vegetable market and the soaked water was obtained by filtering dreg out of the mixture for NaNO2 detection. All the analytical results were summarized in Table 2 and the standard addition method was used to calculate the recovery. It can be seen that the sample solution gave an obvious catalytic peak (curve b of Fig.9A and B) and the addition of standard solution resulted in the increase of the reduction peak at the same reduction potential (curve c to e of Fig.9A and B), proving that in the sample solution the analytical target was contained and the concentration was calculated by the calibration curve. By using standard addition method satisfactory recoveries were got from 101.50% to 104.83% for TCA and from 99.43% to 104.66% for NaNO2, including the practical applications of this method.

Pos sible interference of foreign ions or compounds on the electrochemical response of Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE were investigated by adding 5.0 × 10−2 mol·L−1 various substances, including Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Na+, glucose, glutamine, lysine, proline and threonine in pH 4.0 PBS. As shown in Table 3 , these species made little interference to the reductive peak current with the deviation lower than ±5%, showing the excellent antifouling and anti-interference ability.

As shown in Fig. 10 , cyclic voltammetric responses of Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE in N2-saturated PBS (pH 4.0) exhibited no distinct changes with 25 cycles, and decreased to 85.5 ± 1.33% for Ipc and 89.3 ± 0.87% for Ipa after 80 cycles. No effective changes of current response observed in the first 3 days when Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE was stored at 4 °C in refrigerator. After 2 weeks' storage, 95.5% of initial current still maintained, which indicated that this bioelectrode had good stability. Therefore B-GQDs is a proper nanomaterial for the immobilization of Hb with its activity kept. The long usage span of Nafion/Hb/B-GQDs/CILE may be ascribed to the good biocompatibility and stability of B-GQDs.

The Hb and B-GQDs were applied to the electrode modification to construct a third-generation electrochemical enzyme sensor. Due to the specific properties of B-GODs including high conductivity, small size with specific area, good biocompatibility and electrocatalytic capability, direct electron transfer of Hb on CILE surface was accelerated greatly. Electrocatalytic activity of this Hb modified electrode to TCA, NaNO2 and H2O2 were carefully checked with satisfactory results, which was used for the sensitive detection of these analytes. Also the real samples were detected by the proposed methods with good recovery, indicating that B-GQDs appeared as a promising candidate for the bioelectrochemical sensor.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21665007), the Hainan Province National Natural Science Foundation of China (2017CXTD007), the Key Science and Technology Program of Haikou City (2017042), Foundation of Key Laboratory of Sensor Analysis of Tumor Marker of Ministry of Education of Qingdao University of Science and Technology (STAM201808) and the Graduate Student Innovation Research Project of Hainan Province (Hys2017-149).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: D. Chapman, Molecular Biology, Biochemistry and Biophysics, Springer-Verlag, Berlin, 2009.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of carbon-based nanomaterials as bioimaging probe, Carbon Nanomaterials for Biological and Medical Applications
Electrochemical Methods, Fundamentals and Applications, Seconded
